images.slide_text: Example Interface Specification (cont.)

images.image_path: C:/Users/alima/OneDrive - University of South Carolina/Research/Working Directory/Blackboard_Accessibility_report\course_content\CYBERINFRA-FALL-2025\Module 4 - Structured Design\_imgs_F24-DSci523-Lec4(1)\slide41_shape2.png
images.slide_text:
“Bad” Example





Stack module interface
Sg_pop sets and gets at same time
How to examine top element w/o changing stack?
Violation and how to fix?
Minimality – separate into s_pop and g_top

images.image_path: C:/Users/alima/OneDrive - University of South Carolina/Research/Working Directory/Blackboard_Accessibility_report\course_content\CYBERINFRA-FALL-2025\Module 4 - Structured Design\_imgs_F24-DSci523-Lec4(1)\slide42_shape2.png
images.slide_text:
Another “Bad” Example



Character input module interface
Sg_getc removes next char from input and returns its value
Want to check if end of token
If not end, must use s_ungetc to put char back
Violation and how to fix?
Minimality – separate into s_next and g_cur
S_ungetc no longer needed

images.image_path: C:/Users/alima/OneDrive - University of South Carolina/Research/Working Directory/Blackboard_Accessibility_report\course_content\CYBERINFRA-FALL-2025\Module 4 - Structured Design\_imgs_F24-DSci523-Lec4(1)\slide45_shape2.png
images.slide_text:
Logging System
What is the problem here?

images.image_path: C:/Users/alima/OneDrive - University of South Carolina/Research/Working Directory/Blackboard_Accessibility_report\course_content\CYBERINFRA-FALL-2025\Module 4 - Structured Design\_imgs_F24-DSci523-Lec4(1)\slide46_shape1.png
images.slide_text:
Logging System
What is the problem here?
Client code must know logging goes to a file at /tmp/logs.txt.
If you later want to log to console, DB, or cloud → all clients break

images.slide_text:
Logging System
Clients depend only on Logger. Implementation is hidden and easily swapped.

Taint analysis
Tries to identify variables affected by user input
Tracks flow of data dependencies in program
If tainted variables are ever passed to sensitive functions, flags an error

images.slide_text:
Domain Digital Certificates
Domain Validation
Verifies the identity of the entity that has control over the domain name
Can be automated
Extended Validation (EV)
Requires more extensive verification of the legitimacy of the business
Wildcard digital certificate
Validate a main domain along with all subdomains


<number>

images.slide_text:
Distributed Trust Model
Distributed trust model has multiple CAs that sign digital certificates
The loss of a CA’s private key would compromise only those digital certificates for which it had signed
The workload of verifying and signing digital certificates can be distributed
CAs can delegate authority to other intermediate CAs to sign digital certificates

26

They are stateless; simple policy table; it filters traffic based on specific criteria

images.image_path: C:/Users/alima/OneDrive - University of South Carolina/Research/Working Directory/Blackboard_Accessibility_report\course_content\CYBERINFRA-FALL-2025\Top Level\_imgs_Final Review\slide30_shape3.png

Permitting or denying network hosts or addresses

images.image_path: C:/Users/alima/OneDrive - University of South Carolina/Research/Working Directory/Blackboard_Accessibility_report\course_content\CYBERINFRA-FALL-2025\Top Level\_imgs_Final Review\slide31_shape3.png

images.image_path: C:/Users/alima/OneDrive - University of South Carolina/Research/Working Directory/Blackboard_Accessibility_report\course_content\CYBERINFRA-FALL-2025\Top Level\_imgs_Final Review\slide33_shape0.png
images.slide_text: Stateful Packet Filters

images.image_path: C:/Users/alima/OneDrive - University of South Carolina/Research/Working Directory/Blackboard_Accessibility_report\course_content\CYBERINFRA-FALL-2025\Top Level\_imgs_Final Review\slide34_shape2.png
images.slide_text:
Intrusion Detection System 
Intruders (worms and viruses) spread in seconds.
A network must immediately recognize and mitigate them.
Firewalls can’t protect against malware/zero-day attacks.
A zero-day attack is an attack that exploits a previously unknown vulnerability

images.image_path: C:/Users/alima/OneDrive - University of South Carolina/Research/Working Directory/Blackboard_Accessibility_report\course_content\CYBERINFRA-FALL-2025\Top Level\_imgs_Final Review\slide35_shape1.png
images.slide_text:
IDSs passively monitor traffic.
IDS-enabled device copies the traffic stream.
IDS analyzes the copied traffic offline (promiscuous mode).
It compares the captured traffic stream with known malicious signatures.
IDS does not negatively affect the actual packet flow.
Intrusion Detection System

images.image_path: C:/Users/alima/OneDrive - University of South Carolina/Research/Working Directory/Blackboard_Accessibility_report\course_content\CYBERINFRA-FALL-2025\Top Level\_imgs_Final Review\slide36_shape1.png
images.slide_text:
IDS cannot stop malicious single-packet attacks from reaching the target before responding to the attack.
IDS often requires assistance from other networking devices, such as routers and firewalls, to respond to an attack.
Intrusion Detection System

images.image_path: C:/Users/alima/OneDrive - University of South Carolina/Research/Working Directory/Blackboard_Accessibility_report\course_content\CYBERINFRA-FALL-2025\Top Level\_imgs_Final Review\slide37_shape1.png
images.slide_text:
An IPS device is implemented in inline mode. 
All ingress & egress traffic must flow through it for processing. 
All packets entering the trusted network must be analyzed. 
L3, L4, and packet payload is analyzed (L2-7). 
IPS can stop single-packet attacks. 
Poorly configured IPS can negatively affect packet flow
Intrusion Prevention System

images.image_path: C:/Users/alima/OneDrive - University of South Carolina/Research/Working Directory/Blackboard_Accessibility_report\course_content\CYBERINFRA-FALL-2025\Top Level\_imgs_Final Review\slide39_shape1.png
images.slide_text: IDS vs IPS

images.image_path: C:/Users/alima/OneDrive - University of South Carolina/Research/Working Directory/Blackboard_Accessibility_report\course_content\CYBERINFRA-FALL-2025\Top Level\_imgs_Final Review\slide45_shape2.png
images.slide_text:
Security Zones
Firewall design is primarily about device interfaces permitting or denying traffic based on source, destination, application type, and other information.
Some designs are simple: one outside network and one inside network.
Public (outside) network is untrusted, and private (inside) network is trusted.

images.image_path: C:/Users/alima/OneDrive - University of South Carolina/Research/Working Directory/Blackboard_Accessibility_report\course_content\CYBERINFRA-FALL-2025\Top Level\_imgs_Final Review\slide47_shape19.png
images.slide_text:
Security Zones and Security Policy Rules
Traffic within a zone is allowed by default.
Traffic between zones is denied by default.



DMZ

images.image_path: C:/Users/alima/OneDrive - University of South Carolina/Research/Working Directory/Blackboard_Accessibility_report\course_content\CYBERINFRA-FALL-2025\Top Level\_imgs_Final Review\slide48_shape2.png
images.slide_text:
Security Policy Rules: Controlling Network Traffic
All traffic traversing a firewall is matched against a Security Policy
A Security Policy consists of one or more Security Policy rules
A rule Allows or Denies access to a packet
Rules are processed in sequence and tell the firewall what action to take with each packet that comes through it
Security Policy with four rules

images.image_path: C:/Users/alima/OneDrive - University of South Carolina/Research/Working Directory/Blackboard_Accessibility_report\course_content\CYBERINFRA-FALL-2025\Top Level\_imgs_Final Review\slide48_shape4.png
images.slide_text:

All traffic traversing a firewall is matched against a Security Policy
A Security Policy consists of one or more Security Policy rules
A rule Allows or Denies access to a packet
Rules are processed in sequence and tell the firewall what action to take with each packet that comes through it

images.image_path: C:/Users/alima/OneDrive - University of South Carolina/Research/Working Directory/Blackboard_Accessibility_report\course_content\CYBERINFRA-FALL-2025\Top Level\_imgs_Final Review\slide50_shape2.png
images.slide_text:
Security Policy Rules: Controlling Network Traffic
Permitting or denying actions 
network hosts or addresses (traditional firewalls)
applications, much more granular level (next-generation firewalls)
If there is a match between the current packet being processed and a rule, subsequent rules are not processed for that packet.
The last two rules of the example below are Default rules (allow intra-zone traffic and deny inter-zone traffic).
Security Policy with four rules

images.image_path: C:/Users/alima/OneDrive - University of South Carolina/Research/Working Directory/Blackboard_Accessibility_report\course_content\CYBERINFRA-FALL-2025\Top Level\_imgs_Final Review\slide51_shape0.png
images.slide_text:
Implicit and Explicit Rules
By default the firewall implicitly allows intrazone and denies interzone traffic
Explicit rules must be created to control all other traffic

Implicit rules; by default traffic is not logged
Explicit rule; by default traffic is logged

images.image_path: C:/Users/alima/OneDrive - University of South Carolina/Research/Working Directory/Blackboard_Accessibility_report\course_content\CYBERINFRA-FALL-2025\Top Level\_imgs_Final Review\slide53_shape1.png
images.slide_text:
Application Identification
Application identification and inspection are not classified based on ports, but based on specific application features
Packet payload is inspected
Ports
25: Simple Mail Transfer Protocol (SMTP) (Email)
80: Hyper Text Transfer Protocol (HTTP) (Web)
443: Encrypted HTTP (HTTPS) (Web)
Many Applications can not be identified by port

images.image_path: C:/Users/alima/OneDrive - University of South Carolina/Research/Working Directory/Blackboard_Accessibility_report\course_content\CYBERINFRA-FALL-2025\Top Level\_imgs_Final Review\slide53_shape3.png
images.slide_text:
Application Identification
Application identification and inspection are not classified based on ports, but based on specific application features
Packet payload is inspected
Ports
25: Simple Mail Transfer Protocol (SMTP) (Email)
80: Hyper Text Transfer Protocol (HTTP) (Web)
443: Encrypted HTTP (HTTPS) (Web)
Many Applications can not be identified by port

images.image_path: C:/Users/alima/OneDrive - University of South Carolina/Research/Working Directory/Blackboard_Accessibility_report\course_content\CYBERINFRA-FALL-2025\Top Level\_imgs_Final Review\slide55_shape1.png
images.slide_text:
Example of App-ID in Security Policy
Security Policy with two rules
A firewall uses a set of instructions to control actions: rules
Rules Allow or Deny access to a packet
Rules are processed in sequence and tell the firewall what action to take with each packet that comes through it

images.image_path: C:/Users/alima/OneDrive - University of South Carolina/Research/Working Directory/Blackboard_Accessibility_report\course_content\CYBERINFRA-FALL-2025\Top Level\_imgs_Final Review\slide55_shape2.png
images.slide_text:
Example of App-ID in Security Policy
Security Policy with two rules
A firewall uses a set of instructions to control actions: rules
Rules Allow or Deny access to a packet
Rules are processed in sequence and tell the firewall what action to take with each packet that comes through it

images.image_path: C:/Users/alima/OneDrive - University of South Carolina/Research/Working Directory/Blackboard_Accessibility_report\course_content\CYBERINFRA-FALL-2025\Top Level\_imgs_Final Review\slide56_shape2.png
images.slide_text:
Application Shifts
Network traffic can shift from one application to another during a session
Even after the TCP handshake has completed, the firewall would report insufficient-data rather than an application name
Once an HTTP GET is detected, App-ID can report the application as web-browsing
As more packets are received, App-ID might be able to further classify the traffic

images.image_path: C:/Users/alima/OneDrive - University of South Carolina/Research/Working Directory/Blackboard_Accessibility_report\course_content\CYBERINFRA-FALL-2025\Top Level\_imgs_Final Review\slide58_shape2.png
images.slide_text:
URL Filtering Log
Attachment of a URL Filtering Profile to a Security rule generates log entries:
Specifically, “alert,” “block,” “continue,” and “override” actions trigger log entries

images.image_path: C:/Users/alima/OneDrive - University of South Carolina/Research/Working Directory/Blackboard_Accessibility_report\course_content\CYBERINFRA-FALL-2025\Top Level\_imgs_Final Review\slide64_shape3.png
images.slide_text:
Next-Generation Firewalls (NGFWs)
Advanced Assurance through Deep Inspection
Combine traditional filtering + application awareness, IDS/IPS, and threat intelligence
Key features:
Application-level visibility and control
Intrusion prevention and malware detection
Encrypted traffic inspection
Cyber assurance link:
Continuous monitoring for policy compliance
Adaptive defense improves trustworthiness
More detailed audit on network traffic
Granular control on allowable traffic

images.image_path: C:/Users/alima/OneDrive - University of South Carolina/Research/Working Directory/Blackboard_Accessibility_report\course_content\CYBERINFRA-FALL-2025\Top Level\_imgs_Final Review\slide69_shape3.png
images.slide_text:
Static Analysis

Static analysis is safer
We are not actually running malicious code
Thus, we do not have to worry (as much) about creating a safe environment

images.image_path: C:/Users/alima/OneDrive - University of South Carolina/Research/Working Directory/Blackboard_Accessibility_report\course_content\CYBERINFRA-FALL-2025\Top Level\_imgs_Final Review\slide70_shape3.png
images.slide_text:
File Fingerprinting

As a first step, fingerprint the files you are examining
This will let you know if they change during analysis
Use md5deep, md5sum, Hex Workshop, etc
When you have completed your analysis, or at various points along the way, go back and check the md5sums to ensure the values have not changed

images.image_path: C:/Users/alima/OneDrive - University of South Carolina/Research/Working Directory/Blackboard_Accessibility_report\course_content\CYBERINFRA-FALL-2025\Top Level\_imgs_Final Review\slide71_shape3.png
images.slide_text:
Virus Scan

Always scan malware with an up-to-date virus scanner
Someone else may have already discovered and documented the program you are investigating
If the code is not sensitive, consider submitting to http://www.virustotal.com

images.image_path: C:/Users/alima/OneDrive - University of South Carolina/Research/Working Directory/Blackboard_Accessibility_report\course_content\CYBERINFRA-FALL-2025\Top Level\_imgs_Final Review\slide72_shape3.png
images.slide_text:
Strings

Sometimes things are easy
First look at the obvious – strings
Strings, Bintext, Hex Workshop, IDA Pro 
Be aware of Unicode

images.image_path: C:/Users/alima/OneDrive - University of South Carolina/Research/Working Directory/Blackboard_Accessibility_report\course_content\CYBERINFRA-FALL-2025\Top Level\_imgs_Final Review\slide73_shape3.png
images.slide_text:
Strings

Be careful about drawing conclusions
There is nothing stopping the attacker from planting strings meant to deceive the analyst
However, strings are a good first step and can sometimes even provide attribution

images.image_path: C:/Users/alima/OneDrive - University of South Carolina/Research/Working Directory/Blackboard_Accessibility_report\course_content\CYBERINFRA-FALL-2025\Top Level\_imgs_Final Review\slide76_shape0.png
images.slide_text:
Diffing
Take a snapshot of a clean system state and a snapshot of a compromised system state
Compare before and after
Pros: Artifacts can be observed easily 
Cons: Can miss evidence that is created during malware activities and erased purposely by malware
Tools: regshot, autoruns

images.image_path: C:/Users/alima/OneDrive - University of South Carolina/Research/Working Directory/Blackboard_Accessibility_report\course_content\CYBERINFRA-FALL-2025\Top Level\_imgs_Final Review\slide77_shape0.png
images.slide_text:
System Monitoring
From a clean system state, record every individual change on system and network traffic that appear after execution of made by the suspicious file
Pro: Can collect all manifested changes
Cons: Often too much information and need to weed out irrelevant data

See notes for citation
76

images.image_path: C:/Users/alima/OneDrive - University of South Carolina/Research/Working Directory/Blackboard_Accessibility_report\course_content\CYBERINFRA-FALL-2025\Top Level\_imgs_Final Review\slide79_shape0.png
images.slide_text:
API Tracing
Hook and record important API calls made by the suspicious process
Pro: 
Can provide visibility into activity beyond the typical file/process/registry/network shown by other tools. 
Cons: 
Often too much of information and need to weed out irrelevant data. 
API-specific interpretation can take a lot of time.
Tools: WinApiOverride, Rohitab API Monitor
See notes for citation
78

images.image_path: C:/Users/alima/OneDrive - University of South Carolina/Research/Working Directory/Blackboard_Accessibility_report\course_content\CYBERINFRA-FALL-2025\Top Level\_imgs_Final Review\slide79_shape5.png
images.slide_text:
API Tracing
Hook and record important API calls made by the suspicious process
Pro: 
Can provide visibility into activity beyond the typical file/process/registry/network shown by other tools. 
Cons: 
Often too much of information and need to weed out irrelevant data. 
API-specific interpretation can take a lot of time.
Tools: WinApiOverride, Rohitab API Monitor
See notes for citation
78

images.image_path: C:/Users/alima/OneDrive - University of South Carolina/Research/Working Directory/Blackboard_Accessibility_report\course_content\CYBERINFRA-FALL-2025\Top Level\_imgs_Final Review\slide80_shape0.png
images.slide_text:
Debugging
Set breakpoints inside the suspicious file to stop its execution at a given location and inspect its state. Can break when it calls to important APIs.
Pro: Provides a superset of the functionality of an API monitor
Cons: Malware will often change its behavior or refuse to run when being debugged, which requires a work-around.
Tools: IDA Pro Debugger, OllyDbg, Immunity Debugger, WinDbg

See notes for citation
79

images.image_path: C:/Users/alima/OneDrive - University of South Carolina/Research/Working Directory/Blackboard_Accessibility_report\course_content\CYBERINFRA-FALL-2025\Top Level\_imgs_Final Review\slide80_shape5.png

images.slide_text:
Determining Process Relationship (pstree) 
See notes for citation
87
From the below output, it can be seen that explorer.exe launched OUTLOOK.EXE, which launched EXCEL.EXE, which in turn invoked cmd.exe to execute malware process doсб.ехе. 
By looking at the events you can tell that the user was infected via an email containing a malicious Excel document.

images.image_path: C:/Users/alima/OneDrive - University of South Carolina/Research/Working Directory/Blackboard_Accessibility_report\course_content\CYBERINFRA-FALL-2025\Top Level\_imgs_Final Review\slide89_shape4.png
images.slide_text:
Examining cmd.exe's command line Arguments 
See notes for citation
88
Inspecting the cmd.exe's command line argument shows that the malicious executable was downloaded via PowerShell.
Malware then adds a registry entry for the dropped executable & invokes eventvwr.exe, this is a registry hijack technique which allows doc6.exe to be executed by eventvwr.exe with high integrity level

images.image_path: C:/Users/alima/OneDrive - University of South Carolina/Research/Working Directory/Blackboard_Accessibility_report\course_content\CYBERINFRA-FALL-2025\Top Level\_imgs_Final Review\slide90_shape4.png
images.slide_text:
Determining the File Path 
See notes for citation
89
doc.6 is running from the same path where it was downloaded and dropped by the PowerShell code.

DLL/code Injection
Load a malicious DLL/code into one or more processes
Run malicious code on behalf of a legitimate process
Bypass host-based security software 
HIDS, Personal Firewall
